coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots modified graphene for full carbon-based potassium ion capacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Shu
Song, Yali
Fang, Yongzheng
Zhu, Kai
Ye, Ke
Gao, Yinyi
Yan, Jun
Wang, Guiling
Cao, Dianxue
coredata.dc:description: Potassium ion batteries or capacitors are a promising technology for large-scale energy storage due to the abundant resource and low cost of potassium. However, the development of stable electrode materials with high capacity, capable rate ability, and excellent cycling stability remains a challenge. Herein, carbon dots modified reduced graphene oxides (LAP-rGO-CDs) are designed and synthesized via a rapid and green microwave-assisted method with l-Ascorbic acid 6-palmitate (LAP) as the reducing agent. LAP-rGO-CDs present enlarged interlayer spacing and faster ion transfer rate owing to the introducing of carbon dots. Serving as a potassium ion battery electrode, LAP-rGO-CDs showed a high specific capacity of 299 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 and excellent cycling stability. Moreover, a LAP-rGO-CDs//AC full carbon-based potassium ion capacitor is assembled and displays a maximum energy density of 119 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 5352 W kg−1. This work demonstrates the potential application of LAP-rGO-CDs for high-performance potassium ion storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Potassium-ion capacitors
Graphene
Green reducing agent
Carbon dots
Electrochemical performance

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51702063 , 51672056 ), Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang ( LC2018004 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2018M630340 , 2019T120254 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the preparation process for LAP-rGO-CDs and the digital picture of desperation during different reaction periods. (b–d) TEM and (e–f) AFM images of LAP (15)-rGO-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XPS spectra of GO and LAP-rGO-CDs. (b) Raman spectra and (c) XRD patterns of LAP-rGO-CDs, VC-rGO, and GO. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The first three cycles of CV curves at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1, (b) the GCD curves at a current density of 1 A g−1, (c) Nyquist plots before and after 5000 GCD cycles, (d) rate performance at a current density of 1 A g−1 (e) contact angle measurement, and (f) cycle performance and Coulombic efficiency at the current densities of 1 A g−1 and 2 A g−1 of LAP (15)-rGO-CDs in KIHBs. (g) Comparison of the cycling performance of LAP-rGO-CDs with previous reported KIHBs anode [1,31–41].

(a) Different voltages intercepted by the KIHBs at different stages of charging and discharging at a current density of 50 mA g−1 (b) Ex-situ XRD patterns. Ex-situ XPS fitting of (c) K 2p and (d) F 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CV curves between 0.01 and 3 V (vs K/K+) at different scan rates. (b) log (i) -log (ν) linear plots. (c) The capacitance storage charge (grey area) of the KIHBs accounts for the total storage charge (blue area) at 5 mV s−1. (d) Quantitative analysis of the charge storage behaviours at different scan rates of KIHBs with LAP (15)-rGO-CDs, and (e–h) same test as above of VC-rGO electrodes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) CV Curves of KICs with different mass ratios of LAP-rGO-CDs: AC under 0.5 mV s−1. (b) CV curves at different scanning speeds, (c) GCD tests under different current densities, and (d) cycling performance under the current density of 1 A g−1 of KICs with the mass ratio of 1:5. (e) Ragone plots of the KICs compared with reported representative hybrid capacitors [44–48].

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots modified graphene for full carbon-based potassium ion capacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin, 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology
College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin, 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Potassium ion batteries or capacitors are a promising technology for large-scale energy storage due to the abundant resource and low cost of potassium. However, the development of stable electrode materials with high capacity, capable rate ability, and excellent cycling stability remains a challenge. Herein, carbon dots modified reduced graphene oxides (LAP-rGO-CDs) are designed and synthesized via a rapid and green microwave-assisted method with l-Ascorbic acid 6-palmitate (LAP) as the reducing agent. LAP-rGO-CDs present enlarged interlayer spacing and faster ion transfer rate owing to the introducing of carbon dots. Serving as a potassium ion battery electrode, LAP-rGO-CDs showed a high specific capacity of 299 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 and excellent cycling stability. Moreover, a LAP-rGO-CDs//AC full carbon-based potassium ion capacitor is assembled and displays a maximum energy density of 119 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 5352 W kg−1. This work demonstrates the potential application of LAP-rGO-CDs for high-performance potassium ion storage.

ce:section.ce:para: The application of renewable energy technology and the development of smart grids require the support of large-scale stationary power systems with low-cost and abundant elements. Lithium-ion batteries would not be the best choice for large-scale energy storage systems due to limited resources and lithium salts’ increasing price. Recently, potassium-ion batteries (KIBs) attract lots of attentions owing to the low potential of K (K/K+ −2.93 V vs. SHE) and plentiful potassium resources [1,2]. Moreover, nonaqueous K electrolytes’ high ionic conductivity promises the rapid K ion diffusion between the electrode and electrolyte. Thus, KIBs are considered as promising economical next-generation rechargeable batteries technology. However, the large K ions radius leads to sluggish ion diffusion in the electrode material, which hamper the rate performance of KIBs. It is desired to explore a capable electrode material with improved rate ability for further development of KIBs [3].

According to the K ion insertion principles, anode materials usually can be divided into three types: intercalation, conversion, and alloying. Although the latter two display high capacities, the massive volume change during the discharge process would lead to the shedding of active materials and capacity decay. Thus, intercalation-type carbon-based materials are emerging as promising anode due to the stable cycling performance and appropriate working voltage. Thanks to its unique layered structure allowing potassium ions intercalation and deintercalation, there have been studies highlighting graphite as anode material for potassium ion batteries [4]. Zhao et al. use the 10% Na-alginate-bonded-graphite as the anode to deliver a reversible capacity of 246 mAh g−1 and 89% retention of the initial capacity after 200 cycles [5]. However, the limited layer spacing of pure graphite makes graphite cannot present the same capacity and rate performance in KIBs as it in lithium-ion batteries, owing to the larger ionic radius of K (1.38 Å) than Li (0.76 Å). Besides, the long diffusion path between graphite layers leads to high ion diffusion resistance and decelerate K-transport kinetics [6,7]. Carbon-based nanomaterials would be a suitable alternative for anode materials in KIBs due to the shorter ion transport pathway [8].

Since Andre Geim and Konstantin Novoselov were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2010, graphene has attracted more and more attention in the field of energy storage [9]. The scale of graphene sheets is in the order of nanometer, much smaller than that of bulk graphite, which shortens the diffusion path of ions between graphene sheets. Increased interlayer spacing is also conducive to the diffusion and transport of ions, contribute to the improvement of electrochemical performance of LIBs [10,11]. However, the prepared single-layer graphene sheets are prone to accumulate, and the reduction of specific surface area makes them lose part of the actual lithium storage space. For K+ with a larger radius than Li+, the stacking of graphene during charge and discharge can lead to a more significant decline in KIBs performance. To obtain higher-performance anode materials for batteries, researchers typically enlarge the interlayer distance by doping heterogeneous atoms into graphene [12]. Wu et al. reported a high capacity lithium-ion battery with a B-doped graphene anode, which presented a much larger capacity of 235 mAh g−1 at current density of 25 A g−1 [13]. Han et al. synthesize a kind of 3D N-doped graphene framework coupled with Fe3C@porous graphite carbon as the anode, which can remain the initial structure during charge/discharge and reach the capacity of 155 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 with the high initial Coulombic efficiency of 73% [14]. However, doped graphene would also suffer the reunion issues and most of doping steps require a long hydrothermal reaction involving strong acids or high temperatures [15]. Beyond that, introducing carbon dots (CDs) would be another effective strategy to enlarge interlayer spacing and avoid reunion [16,17]. The conjugated SP [2]/SP3 hybrid structure of the carbon dots, the rich surface/edge center gives it excellent electron transport properties [18,19]. It remains challenging to develop an environmentally friendly approach to synthesize the graphene-carbon dots anode material of KIBs.

To large-scale prepare graphene and its composite, solvothermal treatment is considered as the most attractive method. The key to achieve high-efficient and nontoxic synthesis, the selection of reduction agent and reaction condition is important. Herein, reduced graphene oxide-carbon dots composite (LAP-rGO-CDs) is designed and synthesized via a rapid and green method with l-Ascorbic acid 6-palmitate (LAP) as the reducing agent. LAP is widely used as a food additive with the full physiological activity of l-ascorbic acid (VC), which is also a non-toxic, cheap, and green reducing agent [20]. Microwave reduction of solution-exfoliated graphene oxide has been introduced as a fast and convenient method for both exfoliation and reduction process [21]. It is of great interest to discover that CDs could form on the surface of the graphene layer during the reaction that graphene oxide (GO) was reduced by LAP under the condition of microwave. Compared with the rGO reduced by l-ascorbic acid without CDs (VC-rGO) under the same reaction conditions, the LAP-rGO-CDs possess larger layer distance and more potassium transport pathways. Therefore, CDs play a critical role in maintaining the initial orderly stacked state during the intercalation/deintercalation of K+. This unique effect results in much-improved capacitance (299 mAh g−1 under 1 A g−1) and excellent cycle performance (withstand 5000 cycles of charge and discharge, while the capacity is no longer attenuated) of the assembled KIHBs. For the intention of verifying the application of this material in the field of rapid charge and discharge, we assembled potassium ion hybrid capacitor cells (KICs). We found that the LAP-rGO-CDs//AC KICs deliver a significantly high energy density of 119 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 5352 W kg−1.

The schematic diagram of the preparation of rGO using LAP as a reducing agent is shown in Fig. 1 a. When mixed with LAP, the GO dispersion changed from bright yellow to dark brown and then black after microwave reaction. Since LAP is a weak reductant, LAP could reduce GO but less thoroughly than VC do, unless it is assisted by microwave, which could be proved by the color changing of the dispersions. The slightly reduced GO with conductivity could absorb microwave more efficiently [22,23]. Meanwhile, the microwave can increase the temperature of the mixing system rapidly and accelerate the reduction. Therefore, the large oxygen group contained in the GO was removed, resulting in its reduction. Under microwave conditions, carbon dots are produced and inserted between graphene layers. Based on previous literature reports, the reduction reaction takes at least three times the VC of GO mass. The quality ratio of LAP to GO should be 8: 1 with a considerable mass of VC. After that, to study the effect of LAP content on reduction, The LAP and GO mass ratios at 15: 1 and 30: 1 were experimented.

TEM image (Fig. 1b and c) displays that the resultant featured a gauzy and wrinkled surface with scattered black spheres. A high-resolution magnified image shows a carbon dot about 10 nm in diameter. AFM images (Fig. 1d and e) reveal that carbon dot and rGO layer thicknesses are about 3 nm and 15 nm, respectively. We define the reduction product with LAP: rGO at a mass ratio of 15: 1 as LAP (15)-rGO-CDs. TEM images of different proportions of LAP-rGO obtained under the same reaction conditions also demonstrate the existence of carbon dots (Figs. S2a–c). The carbon dots in the LAP (8)-rGO with low LAP concentration are not obvious but are aggregated in the LAP (30)-rGO. The mapping image in Figure S2 (d-f) shows that the composition of black spheres is basically carbon. To observe more intuitively, we establish a control group by using VC as a reducing agent in the same preparation process. The rGO prepared by VC named VC -rGO displayed a smooth and clean surface with a layer thickness of 4 nm. However, the carbon dots are absent in the VC -rGO (Fig. S3). As shown in the nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. S1). The BET surface area of LAP (15)-rGO-CDs and VC-rGO are 68.12 m2 g−1 and 18.64 m2 g−1, respectively. The rGO containing carbon dots has a larger specific surface area than those without carbon dots, suggesting carbon dots prevent rGO stacking. In view of the similar reduction mechanism of LAP and VC, the reason why LAP (15)-rGO-CDs is thicker than VC -rGO should be caused by carbon dots formation.

To explore the principle of LAP reduction of GO and the reason for the formation of carbon dots, a series of structural characterization methods were applied. In C 1s signal of LAP-rGO-CDs, the peak located at 284.5, 284.9, 285.8, and 287.3 eV corresponded to CC, C–C, C–O–C, and O–CO bond, respectively (Fig. 2 a). There are a lot of C–O–C bonds between the graphite oxide flakes. The intensity of this peak in LAP-rGO-CDs is greatly reduced. The area of CC increases concurrently, which means that GO is reduced successfully. Additionally, the peak of carboxyl groups does not change significantly because most carboxyl groups exist on the edge of GO and are difficult to remove. We found that there are more C–C bonds in LAP-rGO-CDs than in GO, which may be due to the special structure of LAP. LAP is generally esterified by palmitic acid with long carbon chains and ascorbic acid with reducing properties [20,24]. Previous studies have shown that ascorbic acid does not remain between the graphene layers after reducing GO, while palmitic acid and carbon materials with oxygen-containing functional groups have a certain binding force at high temperatures [25–27]. Thus, the mechanism of LAP reduction of GO is shown in Fig. S4. Under microwave action, LAP generates free ascorbic acid radicals, which breaks part of the C–O–C bonds. Simultaneously, the long carbon link at the other end branches to the C–OH bond, and decarboxylation occurs to form carbon dots. It can also be seen in Raman spectra (Fig. 2b). The peaks around 1590 cm−1 and 1360 cm−1 were assigned to G and D bands, respectively. The ID/IG value of LAP (15)-rGO-CDs (1.17) is higher than that of VC (8)-rGO (0.98) and GO (0.95), manifesting that the disorder degree and structural defect increased, which proves the point mentioned above [28,29]. XRD patterns again confirmed that LAP could reduce GO well (Fig. 2c). The diffraction peak of the graphite oxide (001) crystal plane near 10.7° completely disappeared, and reduced resultants showing weak, broad features indicative of few-layer graphene [30]. These changes symbolize that interlayer exfoliation occurs, the size of the single sheet is reduced, the integrity of the crystal structure is weakened, and the degree of disorder is increased. Therefore, the addition of LAP, while reducing GO, also introduces carbon dots between graphene layers. These carbon dots increase the interlayer spacing, allowing ions with a larger ion radius to enter so that LAP-rGO-CDs can be used as a promising electrode material for potassium ion batteries.

LAP-rGO-CDs shows high capacitance and promising cycling stability characterized as anodes and assembled with potassium sheets into potassium-ion half cells (KIHBs). Fig. 3 a displays cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1 between a voltage range from 0.01 to 3 V (vs. K+/K). The reduction peak at 0 V represents the reduction of K+ to K- intercalated-compounds. In the first cycle, an obvious reduction peak around 0.7 V was attributed to the formation of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film due to the decomposition of the electrolyte and the occurrence of other irreversible side reactions. A small reduction peak at 1.6 V appeared in the first scan. The intensity of this peak decrease in subsequent scans, but it still exists. The reason for this change is that partially-reversible side reactions occur between K+ and surface functional groups of LAP (15)-rGO-CDs. The galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) tests show the initial discharge and charge capacities were 1290 and 396 mAh g−1 for LAP (15)-rGO-CDs (Fig. 3b). The low initial Coulomb efficiency (30.6%) is caused by the formation of SEI [35]. To investigate the rate performance of the materials, the KIHBs were tested under different current densities (Fig. 3d, Fig. S5a). High specific capacity of 832, 679, 555, 411, 299, 163, and 54 mAh g−1 was obtained at 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5 A g−1, respectively. Significantly, the LAP-rGO electrodes exhibit stable reversible-capacity when the current densities return to the former rate, suggesting the good stability and reversibility of LAP (15)-rGO-CDs as the anode of KIHBs. Meanwhile, LAP (15)-rGO-CDs displays outstanding cycling performance at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 3f. High average specific capacities of 299 and 191 mAh g−1 with 100% capacity retention rate of more than 5000 cycles were obtained at 1 A g−1 and 2 A g−1, respectively.

EIS and SEM before and after 5000 charge-discharge cycles were measured (Fig. 3c, Fig. S6) [42]. The charge transfer impedance (Rct) is as high as 1600 Ω in the original inactivated KIHBs, and it is reduced to 550 Ω after 100 cycles of GCD cycles due to the activation process. The Rct rise to 680 Ω after 5000 cycles, as the result of the decomposition of the electrolyte and the stacking of LAP-rGO-CDs edges during the long cycle. It can be confirmed by the SEM image of the anode after 5000 GCD cycles that the LAP-rGO-CDs is partially damaged compared to the initial tight state. The EDS results on the electrode surface proved that potassium-carbon compounds were formed on the electrode surface during charge and discharge. The contact angle test shows the electrolyte can infiltrate the LAP-rGO-CDs anode within 86 ms (Fig. 3e). Moreover, the small initial solid-liquid interface angle (14.5°) indicates the favorable infiltration of electrolyte to LAP, which is beneficial to the K+ transfer in the interface between electrolyte and electrode. A comparison with other previous work on KIBs materials is shown in Fig. 3g. Compared with other potassium ion battery materials, LAP (15)-rGO-CDs shows impressive K+ storage performance and excellent cycle stability.

For comparison, the contact angle test of VC-rGO was performed (Fig. S5b). The electrolyte droplet infiltrates the electrode within 273 ms, while the contact angle decreases from 17.2° to 0°. Besides, using as the anode material of the KIHBs, VC-rGO only provides an initial capacity of 177 mAh g−1 and drops to 15 mAh g−1 after 500 GCD cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1, showing poorer performances than LAP-rGO-CDs anode (Figs. S5c and d). The reason for this situation may owing to the fact that the layer distance of VC-rGO is too small to hold a large number of potassium ions, and the graphene layers are easy to stack after cycling charge and discharge, which lead to less transmission path of K+, resulting in low capacity. It can be seen that the presence of carbon dots enlarges the interval between graphene layers, leading to the high performance of KIHBs. LAP-rGO-CDs electrodes obtained by different ratios of LAP was tested at 1 A g−1 (Figs. S5e and f). The LAP (8)-rGO-CDs and LAP (30)-rGO-CDs display discharge capacities of 200 mAh g−1 and 13 mAh g−1, respectively. Lower capacities were attributed to that insufficient amount of LAP is not enough to form adequate carbon dots and enlarge the interlayer spacing after GO reduction, while excessive LAP lead to CDs aggregate and occupy the ion channels of K+. The electrochemical performances correspond to the result of the previous SEM test.

To further explore the mechanism of K ion storge and formation of SEI in the LAP-rGO-CDs, the LAP-rGO-CDs anode under different voltages was tested by Ex-situ XRD (Fig. 4 b), Ex-situ XPS (Fig. 4c and d), and Ex-situ Raman spectra (Fig. S7). The voltages corresponding to the inflection points of GCD curves and cutoff voltages (2.5, 2, 1.5, 1, 0.5, 0.01 V) were selected (Fig. 4a). During the process of discharging from the initial voltage to 1.5 V, few K-intercalated compound is formed, which also explains why the discharge curves barely shows capacity in this voltage range. The characteristic peaks of KC24 appear in the XRD patterns when discharged to 1 V and 0.5 V. When the discharge reaches 0.01 V, the characteristic peaks of KC8 and KC24 are detected simultaneously. The formation of these compounds during the potassition process allows K+ stored in LAP-rGO-CDs. This corresponds to the reduction peak of the CV curve at 0 V. Furthermore, the formation of KC8 and KC24 also explains the high specific capacity obtained between 0.5 V and 0.01 V displayed in the GCD curve. Along with charging, K+ de-intercalate from LAP, which is reflected in the gradual disappearance of the characteristic peaks of KC8 and KC24 in the XRD patterns. The reversible intercalation reaction of K+ between graphene layers allows LAP-rGO-CDs to obtain high capacity in KIHBs. The characteristic peak of KPF6 at 22.5° is caused by the incomplete removal of the electrolyte. The peak with low intensity appears around 31° when the voltage drops to 1.5 V is attributed to the partially reversible reaction of K+ and surface functional groups. The blue area in Fig. 4b represents the formation of SEI film [21]. From the Ex-situ XRD test results, it can be seen that the SEI is already formed when the battery is assembled, and the characteristic peak of the SEI shifts to a small angle as the amount of potassium inserted increases. According to Bragg’s law, this situation may be caused by the lattice expansion of the inorganic phase in SEI. When potassium ions are deintercalated, the peak position does not change significantly, indicating that the SEI film is mainly formed during the first discharge and tends to be stable afterwards.

To realize the specific composition of SEI film, XPS testing was carried out, as shown in Fig. 4c and d. After fully discharge, the peak of K 2p core energy level is observed at ∼ 292–299 eV, showing spin splitting. The peaks at 297.5 eV and 294.7 eV belong to the split peak of K 2p1/2 and the peaks appearing at binding energy of 293 eV and 295.8 eV are due to K 2p3/2. The peaks of C–O–K/COOK are near 296 eV, attribute to the formation of SEI. The high resolution F 1s XPS shows that PVDF exists as a binder on the electrode surface before and after potassition. In addition, K–F bonds indicate the residual electrolyte on the electrode surface (e.g., KF, KF5 −), which is a common phenomenon whereas KPF6 is used as the electrolyte salt. The XPS spectra further prove the existence of SEI film. The co-existence of the PVDF binder and a thick SEI layer on the surface of LAP-rGO-CDs acts as the protective barrier. On the one hand, the formation of the SEI film consumes some potassium ions, which lead to the low initial Coulombic efficiency; on the other hand, the SEI film is insoluble in organic electrolyte, it can protect the layer structure from damage by obstructing the solvent molecules, thus greatly improving the cycle performance of the electrode. The Ex-situ Raman spectra (Fig. S7) indicate the stable structure of LAP-rGO-CDs. As the KIHBs is discharged to 0.01 V, the amount of K in LAP reaches the maximum value. The ID/IG value rises from the initial 1.01 to 1.07 caused by the formation of intercalation compound and drops to 1.03 after the potassium deintercalation. The values can be stably maintained at 1.03 in the subsequent GCD cycles, which proves that the LAP-rGO-CDs can withstand the insertion of large diameter ions without falling apart. It is logical that KIHBs still provide a high capacity after 5000 GCD cycles.

To get an insight into great performance of LAP-rGO-CDs, a series of kinetic analyses were carried out. In CV curves (Fig. 5 a), the peak current (i) and the scanning rate (v) obeyed the following formula:
[1]
i = a
v b
in which a and b are fitting parameters, and the value of b is between 0.5 and 1.0. When b closes to 1, the charge storage of the cell is mainly controlled by capacitance behavior, and when b closes to 0.5, it is mainly controlled by the semi-linear diffusion process [43]. The b value can be calculated by log (i) -log (ν) linear, the b value of cathodes was 0.84 of cathode, and 0.75 of anodes (Fig. 5b). This result indicated the K+ storage in LAP-rGO electrodes was controlled jointly by capacitance and battery behaviours. To analyse the ratio of capacitance behaviour and the diffusion process, the following formula was also used:
[2]
i
( V )
=
k c
v +
k b
v
1 / 2
in which i (V) is the current at a specific voltage, and v is the scan rate. The kc v and kb v 1/2 represent the capacitance behavior and the diffusion process, respectively. The ratios of the capacitance behavior increased from 24% to 61%, with the scanning speed from 0.2 to 5 mV s−1 (Fig. 5c and d). Meanwhile, the anodic and cathodic b values of VC-rGO are also calculated to be close to 0.5, and the capacitance behavior ratio is smaller than that pf LAP (15)-rGO-CDs anode at the same sweep speed. (Fig. 5e–h) Such results also demonstrate that the presence of carbon dots facilitates the rapid adsorption and desorption of potassium ions, resulting in excellent rate capability of LAP (15)-rGO-CDs. Thus, the favorable rate performance and cycling stability could be attributed to the higher capacitance proportion. This fast K+ transfer speed implies that the LAP-rGO-CDs is a promising electrode material for both K+ capacitors and K+ full batteries.

Potassium ion hybrid capacitor cells (KICs) are assembled with activated carbon, a traditional capacitive material as cathode and the LAP-rGO-CDs as anode. The CV curves of AC display its capacitive properties in KIHBs, as shown in Fig. S8. The mass ratios of LAP-rGO-CDs to AC are adjusted to 1: 2, 1: 5, 1: 7, 1: 10 (Fig. 6 a and b). The KICs with the mass ratio of 1:5 shows most regular rectangle, and the shape does not change obviously at different sweep speeds, representing the domination of capacitance behaviour. Further electrochemical studies on the LAP-rGO-CDs (5)//AC KICs are carried out to evaluate its electrochemical performance. The GCD curves at different current densities (Fig. 6c) indicates the remarkable rate performance. We use the following formula to calculate the specific capacitance:
[3]
C m
=
4 i × Δ t
m × Δ V

In which C
m (F g−1) is the specific capacitance of KICs; I is current (A); Δt is discharge time (s), m is quality of active materials (g), ΔV isPotential window (V). The fabricated device delivers high capacities of 400, 361, 360, 300 F g−1 at the current density of 0.5, 1, 2, 5 A g−1, respectively. The retention rate of KICs can reach 90% after 1000 cycles at 1 A g−1, while the Coulomb efficiency does not fluctuate sharply (Fig. 6d). Unsurprisingly, the (VC-rGO)//AC KICs without carbon dots only delivered capacities of 8, 5, 4, 4 F g−1 at the current density of 0.5, 1, 2, 5 A g−1, respectively, and the maximum energy density is much less than that of LAP-rGO-CDs (5)//AC KICs (Fig. S9). The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) of the KICs were calculated according to the following equations:
[4]
E =
1 2
C m
× Δ
V 2
×
1 3.6
[5]
P =
E
Δ t
× 3600
Wherein, Cm: specific mass capacitance of the KICs (F g−1), i: current (A), Δt: discharge time (s), m: the mass of the active material on the electrode (g), ΔV: Potential window (V) [49]. We found that the LAP-rGO-CDs (5)//AC KICs delivers significantly high energy densities of 119, 107.4, 107.1, 89.2 Wh kg−1 corresponding to the power densities of 535.5, 1071, 2142, 5352 W kg−1, which are obviously higher than that of the KICs reported previous (Fig. 6e).

In summary, LAP-rGO-CDs are successful prepared by a fast and green microwave-assisted method with LAP reduction agent. The existence of carbon dots enlarges the graphene layer spacing and provides more active sites, which are propitious for electrolyte infiltration in the electrode and fast adsorption and desorption of K+. Serving as PIBs anode, LAP-rGO-CD shows a high reversible capacity of 299 mAh g−1 under 1 A g−1 and a stable capacity retention rate. The outstanding rate performance is attributed to capacitance behavior. Ex-situ characterizations demonstrate that K+ can be embedded between LAP-rGO-CDs layers to form KC24 and KC8 during discharge, and reversible deintercalation occurs when charged, which promise the high capacity of LAP-rGO-CDs. Moreover, the LAP-rGO-CDs (5)//AC KICs deliver remarkable energy densities and power densities. The strategy of introducing carbon dots can be applied in other 2D materials for metal ion batteries with high capacity, long cycle life and outstanding rate performance.

Graphite (99.95%, 400 mesh, MW = 12.01), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), l-Ascorbic acid (VC, 99%, MW = 176.12), l-Ascorbic acid 6-palmitate (LAP, 98%, MW = 414.53), were obtained from Macklin and used without further purification. GO is prepared by the improved Hummers’ method. The prepared GO was freeze-dried to remove moisture and then used for subsequent experiments.

100 mg GO was added to 100 mL alcohol and sonicated for 4–5 h until a bright yellow homogeneous dispersion (1 mg mL−1) was formed. 4 g LAP was dissolved into alcohol at 25 °C to form a solution at a concentration of 40 mg mL−1 under stirring. Ascorbic acid palmitate was uniformly dispersed the L-ascorbic palmitate in an alcohol solution at a concentration of 40 mg mL−1. The two dispersion solutions were mixed at a mass ratio of LAP: GO = 15: 1 and stirred for 24 h until the solution turned dark brown. The mixture was transferred to a microwave reactor passing through argon earlier. After that, set microwave power to 300 W for 5 min to allow GO to be reduced in argon atmosphere, and kept the mixture boiling during the reaction progress at the meanwhile. The suspension was cooled to room temperature and filtered to obtain a black bulk product, which was washed several times with deionized water and alcohol. Then the LAP (15) -rGO-CDs was obtained after drying at 80 °C under vacuum for 12 h. Besides, the LAP (8)-rGO-CDs, LAP (30)-rGO-CDs were also synthesized. VC (8)-rGO was fabricated under the same conditions while the mass ratio of VC: GO = 8:1.

The micro morphologies of LAP-rGO and VC-rGO were observed by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, JSM7500F), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100), and atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker, Dimension icon). The specific surface area of the product is measured by Brunner-Emmet-Teller measurements (BET, Micro, TriStar II Plus 2.02). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermofisher Escalab Xi+, Al Kα radiation from a monochromatic X-ray source and a 500 μm light spot size) were performed for the surface analysis. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 type) in the range of 5–90° with filtered Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) and Raman spectroscopy (Horiba Jobin-Yvon, LabRAM HR800c) was tested to verify the reduction of graphene oxide.

KIHBs was assembled as CR2032 coin type half-cells. 80 wt% LAP-rGO-CDs, 10 wt% Super-P, and 10 wt% PVDF diluted in NMP were mixed evenly by magnetic stirring to form a black slurry. The slurry was poured onto the copper foil and scraped to a thickness of 300 μm. The copper foil was vacuum dried under 60 °C and cut to the appropriate size. Selected mass loading of active material around 0.5 mg cm−2 as the working electrode, used pure potassium sheet as the counter electrode, dropped 0.8 M KPF6 in DC and DEC mixture with a volume ratio of 1:1 as the electrolyte. Working electrode and counter electrode were separated by a glass fiber membrane, and half-cells were assembled in argon glove box.

Potassium ion hybrid capacitor cells (KICs) were assembled in CR2032 coin cell. The LAP-rGO-CDs and the AC electrode were used as two electrodes of the KICs. Before the capacitor cells are assembled, the AC and LAP-rGO-CDs were pre-activated for five cycles at a current density of 20 mA g−1 in potassium half-cells.

The cyclic voltammetry curves (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, 100 kHz-0.01 Hz) were measured on Bio-Logic VMP3 electrochemical workstation. The galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at different current densities and cycling performance were carried out on NEWARE battery test system. The voltage range of LAP-rGO-CDs KIHBs was 0.01–3 V (Note: All the voltages in this work were compared to the K/K+). The voltage window of the assembled LAP-rGO-CDs (5)//AC KICs was 1–4 V, in which the specific capacitance, capacitance retention, b value, energy density, and power density were calculated.

Shu Dong: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yali Song: Writing – review & editing. Yongzheng Fang: Formal analysis. Kai Zhu: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Ke Ye: Formal analysis. Yinyi Gao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jun Yan: Formal analysis. Guiling Wang: Formal analysis. Dianxue Cao: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51702063, 51672056), Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang (LC2018004), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M630340, 2019T120254) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University.

ce:section.ce:para:
